questions.question_text: A salesperson leaves her brokerage to join a new one. Under Neb. Rev. Stat. § 81-885.20, what must her former employing broker do?
questions.correct_answer: Immediately forward her license to the Commission
distractors: Hold her license until the new broker requests it
Deliver the license directly to the new broker
Nothing — only the salesperson notifies NREC
questions.explanation: When a salesperson or associate broker leaves a broker's employ, § 81-885.20(2) requires the employing (former) broker to immediately forward the licensee's license to the Commission and provide any information NREC requires about the termination. The transfer to the new brokerage is then completed through NREC's Licensee Portal with approval from both the licensee and the new employing broker and a transfer fee of up to $50 (§ 81-885.20(3)). Under 299 NAC ch. 1-011, the licensee is on inactive status from the date employment terminates until the transfer is complete.

questions.question_text: A Nebraska broker opens a second office location. What must she do with NREC?
questions.correct_answer: Obtain a branch office license from NREC
distractors: Obtain a separate broker license
File for a new business entity
No action is required
questions.explanation: A broker who maintains more than one place of business must obtain a branch office license for each branch (Neb. Rev. Stat. § 81-885.19(2)). The Commission issues the branch office license upon payment of an annual fee of up to $50, and the branch must be managed by the broker or an associate broker. A separate broker's license is not required for the location.

questions.question_text: A Kansas licensee wants to practice in Nebraska. What must she provide to NREC?
questions.correct_answer: License copy, fee, and 3-hour NE course
distractors: Only a standard fee and application
Kansas exam scores and official transcripts
A recommendation letter from Kansas KREC
questions.explanation: Nonresident applicants must submit a certified copy of their out-of-state license, pay the required fee, complete a 3-hour Nebraska law course, and pass a background check. Nebraska does not have formal reciprocity with any state.

questions.question_text: Does Nebraska have formal reciprocity agreements with neighboring states?
questions.correct_answer: No, Nebraska has no formal reciprocity
distractors: Yes, with all bordering states
Yes, with Iowa and Kansas only
Yes, through a regional compact
questions.explanation: Nebraska does not maintain formal reciprocity agreements with any state. Nonresident licensees must complete the full nonresident application process including a certified license copy, fees, the 3-hour NE law course, and a background check.

questions.question_text: A licensee fails to complete her CE before the November 30 deadline. What happens to her license?
questions.correct_answer: It is placed on inactive status
distractors: It is automatically revoked
She receives a 90-day extension
A $500 late fee is assessed
questions.explanation: A licensee who has not completed continuing education cannot renew on active status (Neb. Rev. Stat. § 81-885.53). She may renew on inactive status, and the license cannot be activated until the deficient CE hours are completed and evidence is filed with NREC (no more than one two-year period's hours must be made up). Failing to renew at all by December 1 automatically cancels the license on December 31.

questions.question_text: What are the E&O insurance coverage minimums required for Nebraska licensees?
distractors: $50K per claim, $150K aggregate
$250K per claim, $500K aggregate
$100K per claim, $100K aggregate
questions.explanation: Nebraska requires all active licensees to carry errors and omissions insurance with minimum coverage of $100,000 per claim and $300,000 aggregate, with a maximum deductible of $1,000. The policy term is 2 years.

questions.question_text: A licensee's E&O policy has a $2,500 deductible. Does this meet Nebraska requirements?
distractors: Yes, any deductible is acceptable
Yes, if approved by the broker
No, no deductible is allowed
questions.explanation: Under 299 NAC 8-004, an independent E&O policy's deductible may not exceed the deductible in the Commission's group policy for that calendar year — currently $1,000 under the RISC group plan. A $2,500 deductible therefore does not qualify. Equivalent coverage must also provide at least $100,000 per occurrence and $300,000 annual aggregate and, for policies written on or after January 1, 2025, a two-year policy term.

questions.question_text: A licensee decides not to renew her E&O insurance. What is the consequence?
questions.correct_answer: Her license goes to inactive status
distractors: She receives a warning letter
Her license is permanently revoked
A $5,000 fine is imposed
questions.explanation: E&O insurance is mandatory for all active Nebraska licensees. Non-compliance results in the license being placed on inactive status. The licensee cannot practice until proper E&O coverage is obtained and verified by NREC.

questions.question_text: Which licensees are required to carry E&O insurance in Nebraska?
distractors: Only designated brokers
Only brokers and associate brokers
Only licensees with 5+ years experience
questions.explanation: Nebraska mandates E&O insurance for all active licensees regardless of license type or experience level. Licensees may obtain coverage through the NREC Group Plan or independently, as long as minimum requirements are met.

questions.question_text: A licensee wants to purchase her own E&O policy instead of joining the NREC Group Plan. Is this permitted?
distractors: No, the group plan is mandatory
Only if NREC pre-approves the insurer
Only for brokers, not salespersons
questions.explanation: Licensees may obtain E&O coverage through the NREC group plan (Rice Insurance Services Center) or an independent policy with equivalent coverage: at least $100,000 per occurrence, $300,000 annual aggregate, a deductible no greater than the group policy's (currently $1,000), and — for policies written on or after January 1, 2025 — a two-year policy term. Independent coverage must be certified to NREC on a Certificate of Equivalent Coverage signed by an authorized insurance representative.

questions.question_text: Can a professional corporation itself hold a Nebraska real estate license?
questions.correct_answer: No, only individuals may be licensed
distractors: Yes, NREC issues the entity a broker's license
Yes, but only for commercial brokerage
Only if every shareholder holds a license
questions.explanation: Nebraska issues real estate licenses only to individuals — under Neb. Rev. Stat. § 81-885.01(12), a licensed 'person' means an individual. A corporation, LLC, or partnership may still operate a real estate business, but it does so through an individual designated broker: the entity files a statement with NREC subordinating full authority over its real estate activities to the designated broker (§ 81-885.01(6)), registers with the Secretary of State, and the broker records the business form and all trade names with the Commission (299 NAC 2-002, 2-003).

questions.question_text: An unlicensed assistant at a brokerage answers phones and schedules showings. A client then asks her to discuss the listing price. What should she do?
questions.correct_answer: Decline and refer them to a licensee
distractors: Provide only the current list price
Share recent comparable sale prices
Offer a general estimated price range
questions.explanation: Unlicensed assistants may perform clerical and administrative tasks such as answering phones, filing, and scheduling. They may NOT discuss pricing, negotiate terms, show property, or prepare or present offers. Price discussions must be handled by a licensed agent.

questions.question_text: Which task may an unlicensed assistant legally perform in Nebraska?
distractors: Showing a property to prospective buyers
Presenting an offer to a seller
Negotiating repair credits with a buyer
questions.explanation: Unlicensed assistants are limited to clerical and administrative duties: filing, answering phones, and scheduling. Showing property, presenting offers, negotiating terms, and discussing pricing are all activities reserved for licensed persons.

questions.question_text: A broker pays an unlicensed friend $500 for bringing a buyer who completed a purchase. What consequence does the broker face?
distractors: No consequence if under $1,000
Only a written warning on first offense
A fine paid to the buyer
questions.explanation: Paying an unlicensed person for performing licensed activities is a specific ground for NREC disciplinary action. The broker could face censure, suspension, revocation, or fines up to $5,000 or the commission earned, whichever is greater.

questions.question_text: A broker wants to operate her brokerage through an LLC. What must be done before real estate business is conducted under the LLC?
questions.correct_answer: File a statement subordinating authority to a designated broker
distractors: Obtain a separate real estate license for the LLC
Post a $50,000 surety bond with NREC
Have every LLC member obtain a salesperson's license
questions.explanation: Nebraska does not license business entities — only individuals (§ 81-885.01(12)). To operate through an LLC or corporation, the members or board must identify a designated broker and file a statement with NREC subordinating full authority to conduct the entity's real estate activities to that broker (§ 81-885.01(6)). The broker records the business form and any trade names with the Commission and the entity must be registered and in good standing with the Secretary of State (299 NAC 2-002, 2-003). No surety bond is required, and members who do not perform licensed activities need no license.
